title: Chapter 4: The Therapy Session
content: The Solarium smelled like lavender and surrender.
Eight patients sat in a circle. Nurse Cross presided, her cardigan a soft cream, her hands folded in her lap. A clipboard rested on her knee like a shield.
"Welcome to Group Processing," she said. "This is a safe space to share your feelings."
Chloe glanced at Mia across the circle. Mia's face was perfectly blank. She had mastered the art of looking like she was participating while her mind was three networks deep.
"Let us start with check-ins," Nurse Cross continued. "Denise, how are you feeling today?"
Denise, the woman who checked her email too much, twisted her hands in her lap. "Better. I think the medication is helping."
"The medication is helping you regulate," Nurse Cross corrected gently. "Remember, the healing comes from within. The medication just clears the path."
*The medication is an emotional lobotomy,* Chloe thought. *But sure. Clear the path.*
"Sarah?"
Sarah, who wanted a divorce, stared at the floor. "I had a nightmare last night. I dreamed I was running through the house and all the doors kept shrinking."
"That sounds like fear of the unknown future," Nurse Cross said. "Change is scary. But you are safe here."
"Am I?" Sarah asked quietly.
Nurse Cross's smile tightened by a millimeter. "Of course you are. That is what Serenity Peak is for."
When it reached Chloe, she did not wait for the question.
"I am feeling great," she said. "Really. This place has been very clarifying."
Nurse Cross's eyebrows lifted a fraction. "Clarifying how?"
"I have learned that if you call resistance a symptom, you never have to address what people are resisting against. It is a very efficient system."
The room went quiet.
"That sounds like deflection, Chloe."
"Does it? I am just sharing my feelings. This is a safe space, right?"
Nurse Cross's pen paused over the clipboard. "Your file indicates difficulty with authority figures."
"My file was written by the people who put me here. That is not a diagnosis. That is a complaint form."
Sarah coughed. It might have been a laugh.
Nurse Cross's eyes moved to Sarah. Held for a beat. Then back to Chloe.
"We will revisit this in individual session. Mia, how are you today?"
Mia blinked, surfacing from whatever server she had been mentally traversing. "I am fine."
"Fine is not a feeling."
"Adequately sedated, then. Is that clinical enough?"
After the session, Chloe found Mia in the hallway, leaning against the wall, tablet hidden under her jacket.
"That was impressive," Mia said. "You basically told the therapist her entire methodology was a scam in group therapy. In front of everyone."
"She asked how I was feeling. I told her."
"You told her that her profession is a performance."
"Semantics."
Mia almost smiled. "Nurse Cross is going to remember you."
"Good. I want to be memorable."
"That is a dangerous thing to want in a place like this."
Chloe leaned in. "That is exactly why I want it."
At lunch, Tiffany was waiting for them at their usual table. She had a tray with actual food on it — not just toast, but soup and an apple.
"I saw what you did in there," Tiffany said. "Everyone is talking about it."
"What are they saying?"
"That you are not scared of her."
Chloe sat down. "I am terrified of her. I just do not show it."
"How?"
"I pretend she is a Twitter bot. Very confident. Very certain. Completely incapable of self-reflection. Once you see her that way, she stops being scary and starts being predictable."
Tiffany considered this. "A Twitter bot in a cardigan."
"Exactly."
"That helps, actually."
That night, Chloe lay awake and replayed the session in her head.
The other patients had watched her. Really watched her. For the first time since arriving, she had not been a new patient to be assessed. She had been someone who pushed back.
And they had noticed.
She reached under her pillow and touched the collection of notes and gifts from the birdbath. The lip balm. The recipe. The butterfly hair clip. The sketch of the woman on the mountain.
She was not alone here.
She had just forgotten what that felt like.
The next morning, Chloe found a new note under her pillow.
It was not from Mia.
It was from Denise — the email-checker. The handwriting was careful, almost childlike:
*"Thank you for what you said in group. I have been here six weeks and no one has ever said it out loud. If you need anything, I am in Room 12. — D"*
Chloe folded the note into her pocket. She now had three allies. Four if you counted Sarah, who had laughed at therapy.
She was building something. She was not sure what yet. But it was bigger than escape.
At 11:00, during Recreational Period, Mia pulled her aside.

"I found something," Mia said. Her voice was low, urgent. "Webber has a second set of books. The financial records I found were for the facility. But there is another account — a shell company registered in Delaware. It is called Haven Holdings."
"What does Haven Holdings do?"

"It owns the property. Serenity Peak does not own the land — Haven Holdings does. And Haven Holdings is owned by a trust. And the trust is managed by..." Mia paused for effect. "Your father's law firm."
Chloe stopped breathing.
"Your father's firm handles the real estate. Webber runs the facility. Your parents sent you here — to a place where their own company manages the property."
"They are profiting from my incarceration."
"Indirectly. But yes."
Chloe sat down on the bench. The world tilted slightly.
She had known her parents were cold. She had known they prioritized optics over her wellbeing. But this? This was not coldness. This was a business transaction.
"I need to see those documents," she said.
"I need a printer."
"We do not have a printer."
"We do. Admin office. Second floor. It is connected to the network."
"You want me to break into the admin office?"
"I want you to distract Nurse Cross long enough for me to print everything."
Chloe looked at the facility walls. At the cameras. At the fence with the electrified wires.
"I can do that."
That night, Chloe sat on her bed and looked at the welcome packet for the hundredth time.
The tier system. Red to Gold. Rules for everything. Cameras everywhere.
But they had missed something.
They had assumed the patients would be too broken to organize. Too medicated to notice. Too scared to fight back.
They had assumed wrong.
Chloe pulled out the butterfly hair clip from the birdbath. She turned it over in her hands. Cheap plastic. A child's thing. But someone had left it for her.
She clipped it into her hair.
Tomorrow, she was going to raid the admin office.
And she was going to smile while doing it.
She touched the butterfly clip in her hair. A small rebellion. An invisible one.
But that was how change started. Not with explosions. With small things that accumulated until the system could not ignore them anymore.
She closed her eyes and listened to the ward breathe.
Tomorrow, she would raid the admin office.
